data.title: CoinFound Annual Report: TradFi x Crypto 2026 Outlook
data.description:
Takeaway

• 2025 was a year in which traditional finance and Crypto moved from "parallel experiments" to "deep entanglement."

• In 2026, the following macroeconomic trends will impact the TradeFi x Crypto space:

  1. The crisis of trust in the fiat currency system and the return of hard assets: Faced with the global debt spiral and the risk of "fiscal dominance," institutions are accelerating the allocation of "hard assets" such as gold, Bitcoin, and commodities to hedge against the erosion of fiat currency credibility.

  2. Geopolitical drive for the implementation of parallel clearing systems: The need to "deweaponize" financial infrastructure is driving blockchain to become an alternative settlement solution independent of SWIFT, and atomic settlement mechanisms effectively reduce trust and counterparty risks in cross-border transactions.

  3. AI Productivity Monetization and the Rise of Machine Payments: AI investment is shifting its focus from computing hardware to economic productivity generation, creating a rigid demand for compliant stablecoins and on-chain automatic settlement by AI Agents to achieve value confirmation between machines. Energy Becomes a Core Asset and Mining Companies Become Infrastructure-Driven: Power shortages are prompting mining companies to transform into "hybrid computing centers." Their scarce power access rights (Time-to-Power) are triggering mergers and acquisitions by tech giants, driving mining company valuations towards data center infrastructure. On-Chain Assets (RWA) Moving from Issuance to Utility: Asset tokenization is entering the "programmable finance" stage. RWA is no longer just a digital certificate, but serves as a 24/7 core collateral, significantly improving the capital efficiency of the repurchase market and overall liquidity. Private lending faces stress tests and a transformation towards transparency: A wave of debt maturing in 2026 may trigger default risks, forcing the industry to shift from a "black box" approach to real-time on-chain transparent auditing based on zero-knowledge proofs (ZK) to avoid a chain of liquidations in DeFi. Infrastructure shifts from fragmented competition to consolidation by giants: The market is entering a consolidation period similar to the telecommunications industry, with payment and financial giants acquiring stablecoin middleware and custodians to secure their positions, eliminate redundancy, and build compliance barriers. Emerging markets shift from speculation to structural dependence: Crypto assets have become a fundamental tool for payments and remittances in emerging markets, and the vast real-world user scenarios make them a core hub connecting TradFi assets and global retail liquidity. • The following are the trends expected in 2026: RWA market experiences structural growth, stablecoins bottom out at a $320 billion market, equities and commodities will become new growth drivers; Stablecoins enter the 2.0 era, from crypto payments to competing for global payment infrastructure; Stock tokenization liquidity may grow rapidly, DeFi integration will be key; Private lending RWA shifts to "asset-driven," and may accelerate differentiation under the pressure of "default" risk; Gold and commodities RWA Ushering in a new era of "full asset collateralization"; RWA liquidity will become more concentrated, and three types of RWA assets are gaining favor with exchanges; Crypto concept stocks are "rising," and DAT is "differentiating" and "concentrating." Figure 1. 7 Major Trends in Traditional Finance x Cryptocurrency Outlook for 2026 Figure 2. 8 Mega Forces Impacting Traditional Finance x Crypto in 2026 Part 1: Overview of TradeFi x Crypto in 2025 1.1 2025 Overview: The Tipping Point of Integration 2025 will be the year when traditional finance and crypto move from "parallel experiments" to "deep entanglement." If 2024 was the year of ETFs, then 2025 will be the year of "TradFi x Crypto backend reshaping." While the prices of native crypto assets remain volatile, blockchain-based clearing, settlement, and asset issuance (RWA) has quietly become a standard business unit for giants like BlackRock and JPMorgan Chase. Capital is no longer solely pursuing Beta returns, but rather the increased capital turnover brought about by tokenization. Key data achieved in 2025 are as follows: • Stablecoin Market Size Growth – Data: As of the end of December 2025, the total market size of stablecoins reached approximately $30 billion, representing a year-on-year growth of over 100% compared to the end of 2024 (approximately $13 billion). Stablecoin Annual Transaction Volume: In 2025, the total transaction amount of all stablecoins on the blockchain (USDC, USDT, PYUSD, EURC) reached $15.8 trillion, surpassing the annual processing volume of traditional retail payment giant Visa for the first time. Settlement penetration rate: In global cross-border foreign exchange (FX) clearing and settlement, "atomic settlement" based on regulated stablecoins accounts for 2.8%, and the penetration rate in the T+0 clearing field exceeds 12%. (Data Source: CoinFound/ Visa Crypto Analytics/ BIS (Project Guardian Quarterly Review)) • Total RWA Size (excluding stablecoins) and Growth – Data: As of the end of December 2025, the total RWA size (excluding stablecoins) exceeded US$2.5 billion, representing a year-on-year growth of over 100% compared to the end of 2024 (approximately US$1 billion). • Rise of Digital Asset Treasury (DAT) and Crypto Stocks – Data: In 2025, Digital Asset Treasury and crypto stocks emerged. As of the end of December 2025, the overall market capitalization of DAT alone was approximately US$224.1 billion. • Crypto ETF Size and Growth – Data: As of the end of December 2025, the total assets under management of global spot crypto ETFs (BTC, ETH, SOL, and mixed RWA ETFs) were approximately $140 billion, an increase of approximately 20% compared to the end of 2024 ($120 billion). 1.2 2025 TradFi x Crypto Key Events Timeline • January: BlackRock's BUIDL Fund and Circle's smart contract achieved interoperability upgrades, supporting 24/7 USDC subscriptions and redemptions, with T+0 settlement becoming a new industry benchmark. • February: The Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) Project Guardian Expanding to Phase Four, Standard Chartered Bank and HSBC completed their first cross-currency repurchase transaction based on a public blockchain. • March: Stock tokenization received regulatory no-objection letters from relevant jurisdictions regarding the "non-trading-hour circulation of tokenized securities." • April: The first quarterly report after the full implementation of the EU's MiCA Act was released, showing a surge in the issuance of Euro stablecoins, surpassing the British pound to become the second largest on-chain lending currency. • May: The Swift Network announced the completion of its production environment integration with Chainlink CCIP, allowing member banks to transfer on-chain assets via existing Swift messaging instructions. • June: The US SEC approved the first ETF backed by a basket of RWA (US Treasury bonds + corporate bonds), marking the official entry of RWA assets into retail US stock accounts. • 7 • August: A mid-sized private lending RWA protocol faced a credit crisis due to opaque off-chain asset audits, prompting the industry to shift towards "real-time on-chain audit" standards. • August: The total market capitalization of gold tokenization surpassed $5 billion. Amid geopolitical tensions, PAXG and XAUt became the preferred safe-haven assets. • September: Payment giant Visa launched a "tokenized deposit adapter," directly connecting corporate bank accounts with public blockchain DeFi protocols. • October: The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission issued guidance on the integration of Security Token Offerings (STOs) and RWAs, allowing retail investors to participate within limits. • November: The total assets under management (AUM) of on-chain government bonds RWAs surpassed the $50 billion mark. • December: OpenAI... An invested energy company announced the issuance of a "Compute-backed Bond," ushering in a new wave of financialization of physical infrastructure (DePIN).

    Figure 3. A Review of Key Events in TradeFi x Crypto in 2025

    Part Two: The Mega Forces Influencing TradeFi x Crypto

    2.1 Macroeconomic uncertainty, inflation, and debt pressures persist, making safe-haven assets outside the fiat currency system more attractive. • The global fiat currency system has entered a phase of "slow credit erosion." – The "fiscal-driven" risk of US debt is intensifying: As of now, US federal debt has surpassed the $38 trillion mark (data source: U.S. Treasury Fiscal Data), and correspondingly, bond interest expenses have exceeded the defense budget, reaching over $1 trillion annually. Some argue that this debt spiral means the Federal Reserve cannot maintain high interest rates indefinitely (otherwise, interest rates would overwhelm the treasury), but lowering interest rates could trigger inflation. To hedge against this "fiat currency credit risk," institutions must allocate to hard assets with lower counterparty risk, such as gold or Bitcoin. Gold is becoming a consensus among central banks for "de-dollarization": gold is no longer merely a traditional anti-inflation tool, but is regarded by central banks as a strategic reserve for "de-dollarization" and "sanction resistance." According to data from the World Gold Council (WGC), global central banks have made net purchases of over 1,000 tons of gold for three consecutive years (2022-2024/25). This purchasing power mainly comes from emerging markets (China, Poland, Turkey, India, etc.). In particular, since Russia's foreign exchange reserves were frozen, global institutions have realized that "if your reserve assets are someone else's liabilities, then they don't truly belong to you." Gold is the only global reserve asset that does not rely on any government credit, thus becoming the preferred safe haven for sovereign funds. Real assets are beginning to become tools to combat "financial repression": In an environment of "financial repression" where inflation is consistently higher than interest rates, holding cash and bonds represents a loss in real purchasing power. In fact, the long-term inflation center is shifting upwards. Structural factors such as deglobalization (supply chain restructuring), energy transition (green inflation), and labor shortages make it difficult for inflation to return to 2%. The ideal target. Therefore, institutional portfolios (such as the 60/40 strategy) are losing appeal, and instead increasing allocations to commodities and scarce resources. These assets are outside the fiat currency system, and their value is anchored to physical supply and demand rather than government credit. • As long as the trend of global debt expansion does not reverse, institutions will continue to shift funds from "paper assets" (bonds, fiat currency) to "hard assets" (gold, Bitcoin, commodities). This is not just about hedging, but also a vote on trust in currency. Under this trend, – Bitcoin continues to undergo a narrative shift from "retail speculative product" to "institutional asset allocation." Traditional financial institutions no longer discuss whether to allocate, but how to achieve large-scale holdings through regulated channels (such as spot ETFs and their options products). – Gold, commodities, and other RWAs will see more innovative possibilities. 2.2 Fragmentation and Parallel Systems: The Implementation of Geopolitically Driven Blockchain Settlement • Geopolitical frictions have led to the fragmentation of the global payment market, making blockchain-based parallel settlement systems a substantial alternative for cross-border trade. • The need for "deweaponization" of financial infrastructure—moving away from SWIFT's single-point dependence: Geopolitical frictions (such as sanctions against Russia and restrictions on the use of the US dollar for settlement in some countries) have exposed the vulnerability of the highly centralized SWIFT network. Central banks around the world have realized the necessity of establishing a politically neutral settlement channel. Geopolitical tensions are forcing many countries to seek payment infrastructures independent of SWIFT, with BRICS countries and surrounding regions accelerating the development of digital currency payment agreements to mitigate the risks of sanctions (Source: BIS Review 2025/12). • mBridge and CBDC The Rise of Multilateral Bridges: Emerging economies are building multilateral settlement bridges through central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). This is the most typical manifestation of "fragmentation"—the world is no longer connected through a single global network, but has formed multiple regional settlement loops. – Atomic Settlement Solutions and Efficiency Improvements: In an environment of declining global trust, counterparty risk has risen sharply. Blockchain's smart contract capabilities solve the trust deadlock problem of "who transfers first." Furthermore, T+0 transaction and clearing solutions will unlock a large amount of funds in the clearing process, improving capital efficiency. Payment giants like Visa are beginning to utilize blockchain to achieve asset tokenization settlement, viewing it as key to increasing the speed of cross-border transactions and reducing the vulnerability of existing systems (Source: VisaEconomic Insights). • In the context of "geopolitical fragmentation of payments," it is foreseeable that: – Interoperability protocols will become the next generation of SWIFT: With The parallel existence of mBridge (central bank digital currency bridge), various bank private chains, and public chains will create countless "liquidity islands" in the financial market. Whoever can connect these islands will become the new SWIFT. The competition and integration between commercial bank tokenized deposits and crypto-native stablecoins may be ignited. Permissioned DeFi will rise. TradFi needs DeFi's Automated Market Makers (AMMs) and lending protocols to improve efficiency, but it cannot violate anti-money laundering (AML) regulations. Crypto is no longer just an alternative asset, but is seen by TradFi as the technological foundation for building parallel settlement systems. The following are the specific impacts of this trend on the integration of Crypto x TradFi. 2.3 AI Investment: From a Simple "Computing Power Race" to "Economic Productivity Generation" • Capital expenditure on AI is projected to surpass $500 billion in 2026, with AI and blockchain deeply converging at the levels of "value confirmation" and "automated execution." • Annual capital expenditure by major US tech companies has grown from $150 billion in 2023 to over $500 billion in 2026, and AI investment is contributing three times the historical average to US GDP (Source: BlackRock 2026 Outlook). These giants' hundreds of billions of dollars in capital expenditure (CapEx) must be converted into revenue. The market is no longer simply paying for "how many H100 graphics cards" but focusing on who can convert computing power into recurring revenue (ARR). This means that investment will shift massively from the hardware layer (Chips) to the application layer (Applications) and vertical models, seeking "killer applications" that can truly replace manual labor and reduce costs. Agentic AI may become central to a productivity explosion. The "Agentic Economy" may once again become a hot topic. AI can autonomously call APIs, book flights, write and deploy code, and generate income and expenditure. This shift transforms the value of AI from "improving efficiency" to "directly undertaking workflows," thereby generating substantial revenue contributions. It's worth noting that the scale of AI development is so large that even changes in capital expenditure at the micro level can trigger a chain reaction in the macro market (Source: J.P. Morgan). Similarly, the AI ​​trend will impact cryptocurrencies: – Native compliant bank accounts and KYC for AI agents will become key areas of discussion. Technically, AI agents can easily generate crypto wallets and use stablecoins for 24/7 resource procurement and economic activities; however, this depends on solving KYC issues. In the future, "compliant stablecoins" that meet the corresponding KYC and AML regulatory requirements may provide "AI..." The "custodial wallet" service will enter this emerging machine-to-machine (M2M) payment market, using agent-to-agent automated on-chain settlement to seize the B2B payment and DeFi landscape. 2.4 Energy is becoming key to AI and Mining • With computing power demand growing exponentially, "electricity" is replacing "computing power" as the new bottleneck in terms of shortage. Whether training AI models with trillions of parameters or maintaining the hash rate of the Bitcoin network, the ability to obtain stable, cheap, and large-scale energy access (Power Availability) is becoming an increasingly important topic. – By 2026, five data centers in the United States alone will each have electricity demands exceeding 1GW (equivalent to a nuclear reactor). It is projected that by 2028, the data center power shortage will reach 19GW, accounting for approximately 40% of total demand (Source: FT/S&P Global). Energy). Mining farms are transforming into "hybrid computing centers," flexibly switching between BTC mining and AI inference using surplus electricity to maximize revenue per unit of electricity. Some mining companies are choosing to tie themselves to AI giants through "long-term lease agreements," forming de facto joint ventures. On the other hand, leading mining companies (such as Marathon Digital and Riot Platforms) are using their abundant cash flow (from the Bitcoin bull market) for large-scale mergers and acquisitions, aiming not only to increase hash rate but also to control underlying energy assets. Energy bottlenecks are forcing Crypto and TradFi to revalue assets and structurally merge: – The valuation logic for mining companies is shifting from Crypto Proxy to Infra Asset: In the past, investors bought mining stocks (such as MARA and RIOT) as an indirect proxy for investing in cryptocurrencies, such as investing in Bitcoin and investing in Bitcoin-related mining stocks. Now, some mining companies are gradually gaining control of core power resources and transforming into… "Hybrid computing centers" imply a more stable discounted cash flow (DCF) model and a higher price-to-earnings ratio (P/E), reducing the impact of Bitcoin price volatility on stock prices. Therefore, they can be revalued using the logic of data center infrastructure or utility stocks. Meanwhile, mining companies may become a stable part of the "energy + computing power" allocation in a portfolio. TradFi and Big Tech companies may further directly acquire listed mining companies. The reason behind this is that grid expansion and the construction of new substations typically require a 3-5 year approval cycle, which tech giants cannot afford to wait for. The approved gigawatt-scale (GW) grid interconnection capacity (time-to-power) held by Bitcoin mining companies has become the scarcest spot asset in the market. Building gigawatt-scale energy facilities requires huge amounts of capital (CapEx), which crypto-native capital cannot afford to bear alone. We may see more RWA based on "computing power energy revenue". The discussion focused on the on-chain trading of future Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) or Carbon Credits. 2.5 Rapid Evolution of On-Chain Assets and the Future Financial System • 2026 is the year of "Programmable Finance," with traditional financial institutions redefining their middle and back-office processes through their own issued on-chain assets (RWAs). – The US GENIUS Act and the European DLT pilot program will enter full implementation in 2026, allowing sovereign debt, money market funds, etc., to be natively issued and cleared on public blockchains (Data source: Crypto Asset Lab 2026). – Asset tokenization shifts from "issuance" to "utility": T+0 The Liquidity and Collateral Revolution: Over the past two years, institutions have primarily focused on "how to put funds on-chain." The focus has now shifted to "what can be done after going on-chain." BlackRock's BUIDL and Franklin Templeton's funds are not merely digital certificates; they are being integrated into the broader financial ecosystem as 24/7 collateral. More similar attempts are emerging in areas such as stock tokenization (DTCC) and gold tokenization. For institutions, the intraday liquidity release in the repo market will be a highlight. The repo market is the "blood vessels" of the global financial system, but traditional processes are inefficient and costly due to overnight risk. JPMorgan's Onyx and Broadridge's DLR platforms demonstrate the significant advantages of blockchain in handling complex intraday repos. • Under this trend: The large-scale onboarding of existing assets will drive significant growth in sectors like RWA; Ethereum and Solana, among other high-performance public chains, are gradually evolving into universal settlement layers for global finance. In particular, privacy transactions will become a focus, with TradFi institutions processing them through Layer 2 or dedicated subnets. Composability will continue to face both challenges and opportunities from compliance: once TradFi assets are on-chain, the biggest attraction is leveraging the "Lego-like" nature of DeFi (e.g., trading tokenized stocks in Automated Market Makers (AMMs)). Compliance requirements will become the most important entry requirement. This could also spur an explosion of "permissioned DeFi." 2.6 Private Credit Enters a New Phase • In the past few years, private credit has experienced smooth sailing; however, in 2026... 2025-2026 may become the first year of stress testing for private lending. A large amount of private lending issued during the low-interest-rate period of 2020-2022 will mature in concentrated phases in 2025-2026. With the base rate surging from around 0% in 2020 to 4% currently, this could lead to increased interest payment pressure on companies. Many zombie companies held by private equity (PE) may be unable to repay their debts, causing a structural jump in default rates from their current "historically low" levels. On the other hand, a major selling point of private lending is its "low volatility," but this is often due to opaque and outdated valuations. As private lending products are increasingly sold to retail investors (through ETFs or BDCs), fund managers will face significant liquidity runs should the macroeconomy slow down in 2026 and retail investors attempt to redeem their investments. If the past boom in private lending provided assets for RWA, then 2026... The potential crisis of 2026 will be a test for the RWA model: – On-chain transparency becomes the "cure": The crisis will force funds to flow to more transparent RWA protocols. Protocols capable of "real-time monitoring of underlying assets" (e.g., uploading cash flow and inventory data of borrowing companies in real time via oracles) will prevail. The market will no longer trust simple "PDF reports" but will demand proof of solvency verified through zero-knowledge proofs (ZK). – Beware of the risk of "chain reaction liquidations" in DeFi lending protocols: If a wave of defaults erupts in the offline private lending market in 2026, the value of these RWA tokens will plummet. This will test the liquidation mechanisms and risk isolation capabilities of DeFi protocols. If not handled properly, offline defaults will spread to the on-chain via RWA, causing stablecoins to de-peg or lending protocols to default. This could lead to regulators imposing extremely strict "capital controls" on RWA. 2.7 Infrastructure Merger Moment: From "Fragmented Competition" to "Giant Consolidation" • In the 2025-2026 cycle, the crypto infrastructure is undergoing a consolidation period similar to the internet and telecommunications industry in the 2000s. Projects are being swallowed up, and giants are securing their positions through mergers and acquisitions. This trend will continue. The market is no longer paying for the "100th L2" or "new cross-chain bridges," and capital efficiency demands the elimination of redundancy. In particular, some trends are evident: – Payment giants will continue to devour "stablecoin middleware": The aftermath of Stripe's acquisition of Bridge. With Stripe's acquisition of Bridge ($1.1 billion) as the starting gun, traditional fintech giants realized that instead of building complex blockchain node networks from scratch, it was better to directly acquire mature "stablecoin API" infrastructure. Vertical Integration of Exchanges and Custodians: As fee wars intensify, pure transaction fee revenue is declining. Coinbase, Binance, and traditional asset management companies (such as Fidelity and BlackRock) are extending into upstream and downstream of the industry chain through mergers and acquisitions. 2.8 Room to Run for Emerging Markets: From Speculative Participation to Structural Dependence • In emerging markets (EM), cryptocurrencies such as Stablecoin and DeFi, along with their related applications, have enabled substantial financial services and are experiencing rapid growth. Some examples include: – Deepening Penetration: In regions such as India and Southeast Asia, cryptocurrencies are not only investment products but also underlying tools for payments and cross-border remittances. 76% of global investors plan to increase their exposure to digital assets in 2026, with emerging markets being a major growth driver (Source: CoinbaseInstitutional/CoinDCX). – Regulatory Compliance: Clearer rules gradually emerging in Asia (such as Singapore's MAS stablecoin framework) and Latin America have cleared obstacles for local institutions to participate in Crypto (Data source: B2Broker). This "structural dependence" in emerging markets provides the broadest "real-world user scenarios" and "asset distribution channels" for the integration of Crypto and TradFi. Emerging markets have become "application laboratories" for global crypto assets. Mature on-chain payment and credit models have first closed loops in these regions, subsequently feeding back into developed markets. Part Three: 2026 Trend Outlook 3.1 RWA Market to Grow 50% Annually, Stablecoins to Bottom Out at 320 Billion, Equities and Commodities to Become New Growth Drivers • Overall, the RWA industry is growing rapidly, with stablecoins still dominating. Non-Stablecoin Categories Within RWA, equity assets saw significant growth, with institutional alternative funds, stocks, and private equity (PE) experiencing explosive growth. Commodity RWA continued its accelerating trend. • RWA market capitalization grew by nearly 50% in 2025. At the beginning of 2025, RWA's market capitalization was close to $220 billion, and by the end of 2025, the RWA market size reached $320 billion. While stablecoins still accounted for the vast majority ($280 billion+), the growth rates of other asset classes are also noteworthy. Figure 4. Market Capitalization Comparison of Various RWA Sectors at the Beginning and End of 2025 (Source: CoinFound) • Among all RWA asset classes, Private Credit and US Treasury Debt ranked second (approximately $18 billion) and third (approximately $85 billion) in market capitalization, respectively. Private Credit accounted for more than half of the market capitalization of non-stablecoin RWAs starting in 2022. Despite the significant rise of US Treasury Debts in 2023-2024, data proves that Private Credit is the dominant non-stablecoin RWA. It has been a long-term cornerstone. Since 2022, it has consistently held more than half of the market share, reaching a market capitalization of $18 billion by the end of 2025. This demonstrates that on-chain lending has been deeply embedded in real-world financing, forming a stable lending ecosystem. Bonds, on the other hand, experienced rapid growth in 2023-2024, thanks to market education through the high-interest environment. • In the non-stablecoin RWA sector, the growth drivers began to shift in 2025: – In 2025, institutional funds and institutional alternative funds, stocks, and private equity saw significant growth in market capitalization share. Furthermore, these categories became new growth drivers in 2025; in contrast, in 2024, the growth of non-stablecoin RWA was primarily driven by private lending RWA and US Treasury RWA. – Furthermore, RWA in the commodities category continued to achieve rapid growth in every quarter of 2025, achieving a 300% increase throughout the year (from a market capitalization of $1 billion to $3 billion), with the growth rate showing a trend of accelerating quarter by quarter.     3.2 From Stablecoin 2.0, from Crypto Payments to Global Payment Infrastructure and Interest Generation

    • In 2025, stablecoins officially shed their single label as "crypto settlement tools" and evolved into the digital capillaries of global financial circulation. As of the end of December, the total market capitalization of stablecoins exceeded $250 billion, and their annual settlement volume historically surpassed the Visa system, marking that the settlement layer based on public chains has the strength to compete with traditional financial giants.

    Figure 11. Market capitalization of various private lending RWA issuance platforms from 2022 to 2025 (Data source: CoinFound)

(Weight), as the "traffic engine" of the entire network settlement, has an extremely high turnover rate and is an extension of the on-chain dollar. – Stock RWA (turnover rate 15%-25%): As a "yield gamer," driven by the volatility of US stocks, it has performed exceptionally well on Bybit and Kraken, and is key to attracting retail arbitrage traffic. – US Treasury bonds and credit (turnover rate <5%): As a "credit ballast," although the trading frequency is low, it can be used as an underlying asset in lending protocols. These assets are gradually evolving into "core on-chain collateral," undergoing a profound evolution from "earning yield" to "collateralization." • Correspondingly, exchange strategies are beginning to diverge: – Leveraging compliance advantages to monopolize the institutional US Treasury and USDC markets: Although Coinbase ranks 5th in trading volume, its weighting is highly concentrated in USDC (84%) and institutional US Treasury (12%), demonstrating its deep cultivation of the compliance and institutional markets. – Shifting towards panoramic exchanges through aggressive RWA deployments to compete for global users: Bybit, Kraken, and Gate.io are very aggressive in their RWA deployments. Bybit, in particular, has RWA as its primary weighting (46%), indicating that these exchanges are attracting retail traffic seeking on-chain trading of US/global stocks. – The power of DEX and DeFi: Uniswap and Curve entering the top 10 signifies that RWA is no longer limited to CEXs. In particular, Uniswap's trading volume ($2.6 billion+) in interest-bearing stablecoins and US Treasuries is comparable to top-tier centralized exchanges. High-turnover assets (stablecoins, stocks) support the exchange's market share and activity; while low-turnover assets (US Treasuries, private lending), although having low "turnover rates" in the secondary market, represent the most stable underlying capital inflow in the on-chain ecosystem. The trend in 2026 will be more low-turnover assets releasing secondary liquidity through "collateralization." Turnover rate (Velocity) is a key indicator for measuring the "health" of asset liquidity. In RWA's research report, turnover rate helps us identify which assets are "real trading hotspots." In this research report, we define asset turnover as: – Velocity = (Daily Volume) (Circulating Market Cash) – High turnover: indicates assets are frequently traded, with excellent liquidity, suitable for speculation or as a short-term payment medium. – Low turnover: indicates assets are mostly held long-term (HODL) or used as collateral, with low liquidity.     3.7 The Rise of Crypto Concept Stocks, the Differentiation and Concentration of DAT

    • Benefiting from the full implementation of the fair value measurement of accounting standards (FASB), listed companies including Bitcoin in their balance sheets are no longer seen as "not doing their job properly," but rather as a standard practice to combat the devaluation of fiat currencies.

    In 2025, the MicroStrategy model was replicated globally, and DAT (Digital Asset Treasury) became a widely accepted corporate financial strategy. Despite a period of declining market enthusiasm, DAT still maintains a significant scale. Taking Bitcoin as an example, as of now, a total of 192 DAT holders worldwide hold over 1 million Bitcoins, accounting for 5.74% of the market share. Besides Bitcoin, other DATs such as ETH, SOL, and BNB are also gradually emerging. "Differentiation" is a key word for DAT: the simple "buy and hold" strategy is no longer a panacea for rising stock prices. The market is becoming more discerning, with funds shifting from a "broad net" approach to a "precise targeting of the top players": * Monopolists of low-interest capital vs. Victims of high-interest debt* The core of the DAT model is not "buying coins," but "arbitrage"—borrowing money at a cost lower than Bitcoin's expected price increase. The market is undergoing a brutal differentiation: leading players like MicroStrategy, with extremely high credit ratings and liquidity, can issue convertible notes at interest rates of <1% or even 0%, thus increasing the "coin content per share" even when Bitcoin prices are flat. Conversely, smaller-cap imitators, due to insufficient credit, face financing costs as high as 8%-10%. This huge difference in the cost of capital allows leading companies to continuously absorb capital for expansion, while smaller companies are forced to sell coins to repay debts under the heavy burden of interest payments, falling into a death spiral. * Active Capital Operations vs. Passive Asset Discounts
    The market no longer views all DAT companies as homogeneous substitutes for Bitcoin ETFs. In 2026, the DAT sector exhibited a dramatic K-shaped divergence. The "Premium Tier" comprises companies that actively create value through capital operations (such as high-level share issuance, low-level share buybacks, and volatility arbitrage), for which the market assigns a 1.5x-2.0x NAV premium. The "Discount Tier" comprises "zombie companies" whose main business is shrinking and which merely passively hold Bitcoin; these are viewed by the market as illiquid closed-end funds, receiving a 0.8x-0.9x discount. This divergence signifies the end of the era of "blindly buying DAT concept stocks," with alpha returns now solely derived from betting on management's capital allocation capabilities. * Simple "Hoarders" vs. Comprehensive "Ecosystem Builders"* To break the discount curse, some DAT (Digital Asset and Bitcoin) companies are seeking differentiated transformations, shifting from "balance sheet holding" to "business flow integration." This has led to the emergence of a group of "DAT" companies. For example, Japan's Metaplanet not only hoards Bitcoin but also uses it for cross-border real estate settlements; the US's Block (Square) has deeply integrated Bitcoin into the closed loop of Cash App. In contrast, companies that have no real business synergy besides hoarding Bitcoin are being marginalized. The market is beginning to reward companies that can make Bitcoin "turn" on their balance sheets (generating turnover or payment utility), rather than simply letting it "lie dormant."* • "Differentiation" is the prelude to mergers and acquisitions. Leading DAT companies with high premiums will use their expensive stock as currency to acquire discounted tail-end companies, stripping them of their Bitcoin holdings at extremely low cost. This will further concentrate Bitcoin holdings in the hands of a few giants in publicly traded companies. In the market, banks and bond investors are also building more complex risk models for DAT companies. For companies at the bottom of the "divergence," TradeFi funding channels will close, forcing them to either delist and go private, or be liquidated or restructured by native DeFi capital (such as DAO Treasury) through lending protocols. It's worth noting that the market is reclassifying these companies as "Leveraged BTC Holding Companies" (buying their stock is roughly equivalent to indirectly buying the corresponding amount of reserve tokens), and their premium to NAV has become a core indicator of market sentiment. Currently, many DAT companies have relatively low premiums, implying potential upside potential. Figure 16. DAT Market Overview as of January 2026 (Data Source: CoinFound) On the other hand, crypto concept stocks continue to rise. If DAT (Digital Asset Treasury) companies are the "asset-side mapping" of cryptocurrencies in the US stock market, then crypto concept stocks (Exchanges, Fintech, Asset Managers) are the "operation-side mapping". In 2026, traditional financial market investors will no longer merely seek Bitcoin's price as a shadow (High Beta), but will begin to demand ecosystem dominance and recurring revenue (ARR), and will differentiate their pricing accordingly. Currently, global crypto stocks are scattered across various industries, but are led by capital markets. In particular, we focus on the following categories: • On the other hand, with soaring regulatory costs and improved infrastructure, the crypto stock market in 2026 will exhibit extremely high industry concentration. Small and medium-sized exchanges and independent wallet service providers will either be acquired by giants or exit due to excessive compliance costs.

    The concept of "Too Big To Fail" has officially entered the crypto stock sector, with leading companies enjoying a significant regulatory moat premium.

    ... Figure 17. Overview of the Crypto Concept Stock Market as of January 2026 (Data Source: CoinFound) The success of RWA (Retail Asset Waiver) for government bonds proves the feasibility of putting traditional assets on-chain, while the full-scale entry of giants like BlackRock provides irreversible credit backing for the industry. 2026 Forecast: 2026 will be a year of "secondary market explosion and credit expansion." We predict: • Liquidity explosion: With the improvement of infrastructure, RWA will shift from "holding for interest" to "high-frequency trading." • Credit saturation: Asset classes will shift from high-credit government bonds to corporate bonds, stocks, and emerging market credit, with risk premiums becoming a new source of returns. • Risk warning: As RWA scales up, the complexity of off-chain defaults transmitting to on-chain liquidation will be the biggest systemic risk. In 2026, both TradeFi and Crypto will be unified under the banner of "On-chain Finance".

data.title: With the Federal Reserve about to change leadership, will your cryptocurrency rise or fall?
data.description:
The names of the four candidates kept flashing on the prediction platform, and cryptocurrency traders' computer screens were flashing hints of their future fate.

US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant revealed at the Davos Forum that President Trump "may announce" the new Federal Reserve Chairman as early as next week.

US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant revealed at the Davos Forum that President Trump "may announce" the new Federal Reserve Chairman as early as next week.

The final shortlist has now narrowed down to four candidates. The market has begun pricing in a possible policy shift by the Federal Reserve. The shortlist of four candidates has been narrowed down to four: White House National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett, former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh, current Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller, and BlackRock Global Fixed Investment Director Rick Reid. Last week, Trump's comments shifted the candidate landscape. He publicly praised Hassett's work and indicated a preference for Hassett to continue in his current position. This shift in attitude has directly impacted market expectations. On the prediction platform Kalshi, Warsh's probability of becoming Federal Reserve Chairman has risen to 59%, while Hassett's probability has plummeted to 14%. According to data from prediction platforms Polymarket and Kalshi, Hassett, Warsh, and Waller are currently the three most watched candidates. These candidates each represent different monetary policy orientations, which will directly affect the global liquidity environment. Hassett is considered the most "dovish" candidate, supporting aggressive interest rate cuts to stimulate economic growth. Warsh, on the other hand, leans more "hawkish," emphasizing maintaining the Fed's independence and prioritizing long-term monetary stability and inflation control. Current Fed Governor Waller is known for his data-driven decision-making style and more flexible stance. The market is holding its breath, awaiting a breakout. The cryptocurrency market is undergoing a cautious consolidation phase, with its total market capitalization currently hovering around $2.94 trillion. Technical indicators suggest the market is poised for a breakout, but a clear catalyst is lacking. Traders' wait-and-see attitude reflects macroeconomic uncertainty. This compression suggests the market is anticipating a breakout, but lacks a clear catalyst in the short term. The correlation between the cryptocurrency market and US monetary policy has been strengthening since 2022. Traders are primarily focused on whether the new Fed chair will bring about a faster-than-expected rate-cutting cycle. The Crypto Effect of Policy Shift If the new Fed chair prioritizes growth and cuts rates faster than expected, cryptocurrencies could outpace traditional markets in a rebound. Historically, Bitcoin's bull runs in 2017, 2021, and 2024 were all supported by a dovish stance from the Federal Reserve or balance sheet expansion. There is a clear correlation between monetary conditions and cryptocurrency prices. Lower interest rates reduce the yields of traditional safe-haven assets, prompting investors to seek higher-risk assets for returns. Analysts believe that if inflation falls while unemployment rises, the Federal Reserve may need to act more aggressively, which would benefit the cryptocurrency market. Cryptocurrency analysts have envisioned various market scenarios based on the different policy paths the Federal Reserve might take. The most optimistic scenario is that a dovish Fed transition would trigger a liquidity rebound, pushing the total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies above $3.2 trillion. In the baseline scenario, gradual easing policies accompanied by stable inflation will cause cryptocurrencies to fluctuate between $2.7 trillion and $3.2 trillion. In the most pessimistic scenario, if inflation rebounds or policy conflicts occur, the total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies could retest $2.5 trillion. Traders should pay attention to the breakout timing of the current volatility compression. Positioning before the policy window from February to May may be key. The technical chart of the total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies shows that the Bollinger Bands have narrowed significantly, with market volatility compressed within a narrow trading corridor of $2.84 trillion to $3.12 trillion. This compression is a classic sign that market volatility is imminent. Traders are watching their screens closely, awaiting the possible announcement of the leadership selection next week. Regardless of the outcome, the next market breakout will not just be technical; it will be political.

data.title: Trump wants to "fix the housing market first, then cryptocurrency," Congress delays crypto market bill.
data.description:
Author: Bao Yilong, Wall Street Insights

The U.S. Senate Banking Committee is shifting its legislative focus from cryptocurrencies to housing policy.

On January 21, Bloomberg, citing sources familiar with the matter, reported that the U.S. cryptocurrency market bill may be delayed for at least several weeks until the end of February or March, as the committee is studying Trump's housing policy to implement his demands.

Previously, on Tuesday, Trump signed an executive order instructing his administration to issue guidance to prevent the government from supporting or facilitating the purchase of single-family homes by large institutional investors. The order also instructed the U.S. Treasury Department to develop a threshold definition to determine which investors qualify as large institutions.

However, the actual impact of this move on housing prices remains unclear. It is estimated that large institutional investors own less than 1% of all single-family homes in the U.S.

Despite Trump and his administration prioritizing cryptocurrency, housing remains the largest monthly expense for most Americans and a major driver of inflation. Following the Republican Party's losses in several key elections late last year, concerns about housing costs are widely seen as a political disadvantage. The shift in legislative focus has cast a shadow over the Senate's efforts to enact new legislation on cryptocurrency market structures. Supporters argue the proposal would clarify the jurisdiction of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) over the crypto industry. Both regulators claim partial jurisdiction over digital assets but say Congress needs to clarify their respective responsibilities. Last week, the legislation was abruptly delayed after Coinbase withdrew its support. This delay may give parties in the financial and crypto industries more time to lobby for a legislative agreement that will garner broad support. The legislative process also faces the complexity of inter-committee coordination. The Senate Agriculture Committee, which also has a say in the final bill, plans to release its version of digital asset legislation later Wednesday and may vote on it on January 27. However, the Agriculture Committee's version needs to be merged with the bill being considered by the Banking Committee before it can be submitted to the full Senate for a vote. This process adds further uncertainty to an already volatile legislative process.

data.title: Bankless: A Comparative Analysis of Two Major Privacy Coins, Zcash and Monero
data.description:
Author: David Christopher, Source: Bankless, Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

During the past few months of cryptocurrency market downturn, privacy trading has become a rare bright spot. Among them, the native token trading activity of Zcash (ZEC) and Monero (XMR) has been particularly active.

ZEC's amazing surge in September pushed this privacy coin from a slump to its peak, and in November it hit a nine-year high, up 28 times from its low point a year ago. While Zcash stole the spotlight, Monero (XMR) was also steadily climbing—although its initial full price was much higher than Zcash. However, two weeks ago, everything changed dramatically: The Zcash core development team—Electric Coin Company (ECC)—announced that they would be leaving Zcash due to governance disputes—ZEC's price plummeted, while XMR found new room for development and regained its dominance in the privacy coin market.

... While Monero has been a relatively large asset by market capitalization for most of its lifespan, the ECC governance controversy has revitalized a long-standing argument within the XMR camp—that it is a “true privacy coin,” fully private, not optional. This debate is now back in the spotlight as both coins, along with the broader altcoin market, have experienced significant declines. Let’s explore the differences between Zcash and Monero—not just technically, but also organizationally. Both claim to solve the same problem posed by Satoshi Nakamoto in 2008, but their paths to achieving this goal have been drastically different. Origin Story Perhaps the fundamental difference between these two projects lies in their origins: Monero was born in a forum, while Zcash was born in a university. Monero was created in 2014 by an anonymous user named thankful_for_today, initially named "Bitmonero," based on the CryptoNote protocol written by the pseudonymous developer Nicolas van Saberhagen. It is well known that the community "took over" the project early on and reversed some of the founders' controversial decisions. Monero has no CEO or office; its development funds come entirely from voluntary donations through the Community Crowdfunding System (CCS). In the CCS, contributors submit proposals, which are openly discussed by the community, and funds are only disbursed after milestones are validated. A small core team manages the project—managing the codebase and escrowing funds—but they do not determine the technology roadmap. Decentralization remains an inherent characteristic of Monero. Meanwhile, Zcash's origins can be traced back to an academic study at Johns Hopkins University in 2013, when cryptographers developed the Zerocoin protocol. This design gradually evolved into Zerocash, eventually becoming Zcash, which was launched in 2016 by cypherpunk enthusiasts Zooko Wilcox and the Electric Coin Company. While the project retains its cypherpunk origins, like Monero, Zcash has consistently cooperated with regulators rather than confronted them. These different origins have resulted in drastically different organizational structures and reputations. Monero's mandatory privacy mechanisms have made it the preferred choice for darknet markets. According to TRM Labs, nearly half of the new darknet markets that emerged in 2024 chose to use XMR exclusively. In contrast, Zcash is rarely mentioned in ransomware or darknet reports. XMR's reputation has led regulators to pressure centralized exchanges to delist the asset. Binance delisted XMR in February 2024, OKX took the same step in January 2024, and Kraken delisted XMR for European users in October 2024. Zcash also faced similar scrutiny but successfully avoided a similar large-scale delisting: Binance removed the token's "monitoring label" in July 2025, and OKX relisted it in November 2025. To understand the technical differences between these two protocols, consider an analogy. Imagine any transaction as a message you need to send. With Monero, your message blends into the crowd—you speak at the same time as 15 other people, so bystanders know what someone said but can't prove it was you. With Zcash's crypto transactions, your message is placed in a "box" that only the recipient can open. Bystanders can't see the information content at all; they can only see the box. Monero employs a three-pronged approach: Ring Signatures: By mixing your transaction with other transactions already recorded on the blockchain (currently about 16, called booby transactions), the sender's identity is hidden. Any one of these 16 transactions could be the real sender, providing a plausible space for denial. RingCT (Ring Confidential Transactions): Hides the amount by encrypting the transaction value, while also proving that no new coin was created. Stealth Addresses: Hides the recipient's identity by creating a one-time address for each transaction. Even if someone knows your public key address, they cannot associate the received transaction with that address. On the other hand, Zcash uses zk-SNARKs to provide privacy protection, verifying the validity of transactions without revealing the sender, receiver, or amount. To do this, when you send a crypto transaction, Zcash generates a cryptographic proof that the network verifies—confirming your right to use those tokens and that the input matches the output—without checking the actual transaction details. Mandatory Privacy vs. Optional Privacy In Monero, privacy is mandatory. You cannot send transparent transactions. This creates "herd immunity"—all transactions look the same, so no one stands out. The argument is that optional privacy is not true privacy. If only "suspicious" people use privacy options, then they become targets—a strong argument.

Zcash offers privacy options. ZEC can be used transparently or moved to a shielded address, where ZK functionality will be used to obfuscate transaction details and status. Users are free to switch between these two methods. Even when not strictly necessary, the size of the shielded pool continues to grow—currently, about 30% of the ZEC supply is in the shielded pool, up from 8.7% a year ago. Monero uses the RandomX Proof-of-Work algorithm, designed to resist ASIC mining and optimized for general-purpose CPUs, making it easy for home miners. Monero also employs a tail-issuance mechanism—an unlimited supply with a small, fixed amount of tokens added continuously (approximately 0.6 Monero per block). This ensures miners are always incentivized to maintain network security. Zcash currently uses Equihash—a Proof-of-Work consensus mechanism optimized for ASIC mining, which tends to favor professional miners. It is transitioning to Crosslink, a hybrid Proof-of-Work/Proof-of-Stake system that brings deterministic transaction finality to the network. In pure PoW systems, blocks are never truly "final"—they simply become statistically increasingly irreversible. Bitcoin doesn't have this problem, but theoretically, it would make much smaller networks vulnerable to 51% attacks and pose significant challenges to secure bridging with other chains. Crosslink adds a proof-of-stake "finality mechanism" on top of proof-of-work: miners continue to generate blocks, but stakers provide a second confirmation, making transactions permanent and irreversible. Like Bitcoin, which it originally forked from, Zcash has a fixed supply cap of 21 million ZECs, with halving cycles approximately every four years. Both protocols have major upgrades under development that could change the competitive landscape. Monero is developing FCMP++ (Full Chain Membership Proof), a major upgrade designed to replace the current ring signatures. Transactions will then no longer be mixed with approximately 16 booby traps, but rather with the entire blockchain history. This will significantly expand the anonymity set from "16 people" to "all people," potentially offsetting Zcash's shielded pool advantage. Zcash is developing Tachyon, a major scaling initiative hailed as "Zcash's Firedancer," which will dramatically improve network speed. Furthermore, Zcash has launched Crosslink for hybrid proof-of-stake transitions and improved user experience with features like a new wallet and Near Intents integration. Another noteworthy technical detail: Zcash's privacy layer is quantum-resistant, while Monero's ring signatures do not. Monero developers are aware of this and plan to address it through FCMP++ and future upgrades. Different tools, different betting methods. Beyond technological differences, a project's success ultimately depends on its adoption—and adoption depends on two factors, which are handled very differently: **organizational strength and reputation.** In terms of organizational structure, Zcash's corporate structure allows for rapid R&D and adoption of cutting-edge cryptographic technologies, but the exit of ECC exposed its concentration risks. The Zcash Foundation and community donations provide support, but whether they can maintain momentum remains to be seen. Monero's decentralized contributor model is slower and more difficult to coordinate, but the exit of any single contributor will not trigger a crisis. Regarding reputation, the trade-offs are equally stark. Zcash's optional privacy and compliance features allow it to be listed on major exchanges, but at the cost of a smaller shielded liquidity pool and weaker network-level privacy protection. Monero's mandatory privacy features made it the dominant currency on the dark web market—a fact that led to its systematic delisting by exchanges and attracted the hostility of regulators. Nevertheless, this regulatory hostility may be more indicative of the product's value than any other factor. Ultimately, the debate over the "only true privacy coin" boils down to what users are looking for. Monero currently offers stronger privacy protection but faces greater resistance to adoption. Zcash offers a more widespread path but requires users to actively choose privacy protection—which most users don't do. While both tokens have revitalized after years of dormancy, they must now prove they can maintain attention and that their technological architecture and organizational support can outperform each other and ultimately ensure long-term sustainability.

data.title: RWA Compliance: Competition and Synergy Between South Korean Legislation and Hong Kong's Sandbox
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

On January 15, 2026, the National Assembly of South Korea passed amendments to the Electronic Registration Act for Stocks and Bonds and the Financial Investment Services and Capital Markets Act (FSCMA), marking the formal legal recognition of security tokens in South Korea and opening a compliant channel for asset tokenization (RWA) businesses. This legislative move not only reshapes the digital landscape of the South Korean financial market but also creates a regional competitive and collaborative situation with the regulatory practices in Hong Kong, providing a dual-track reference for the development of asset tokenization in East Asia.

I. Analysis of the Core Content of the Amendments to the Two Laws in South Korea

This amendment in South Korea is not to establish a parallel regulatory system for security tokens,but rather to embed them into the existing capital market framework by revising the two core laws, achieving "compliance expansion" rather than "institutional restructuring". This approach ensures the stability of legal connections while reserving space for the application of blockchain technology in the securities field. The core revisions focus on three dimensions. (I) The Electronic Registration Law Establishes the Legal Status of Distributed Ledgers The most crucial breakthrough of the revised Electronic Registration Law is that it clarifies the legal status of distributed ledgers as statutory securities registers, allowing for the first time, at the institutional level, the native issuance of securities in tokenized form, rather than the "on-chain mapping" of traditional electronic securities. Previously, securities registration in South Korea could only be completed through the centralized Korea Securities Depository and Clearing House (KSD), with blockchain technology serving only as an auxiliary recording tool. Under the new regulations, issuers can, according to legal procedures, declare to the KSD and directly issue security tokens based on the blockchain, achieving a dual architecture of "direct on-chain issuance + compliant registration." This change not only activates the application scenarios of smart contracts in securities trading but also promotes the upgrading of account management models. The legislation introduces an "issuer account management institution" system, acting as a buffer node between on-chain transactions and the regulatory system. This institution is responsible for aggregating fragmented on-chain data, implementing KYC/AML obligations, and connecting to the central registration system, thus ensuring transaction efficiency while strengthening risk prevention and control. The tamper-proof and traceable characteristics of blockchain will effectively adapt to securities with flexible rights structures, such as trust beneficiary certificates and split investment products, providing technological empowerment for asset securitization. (II) The FSCMA relaxes restrictions on the circulation of investment contract securities. The revision of the Financial Investment Services and Capital Markets Act focuses on expanding the scope of tradable securities, explicitly allowing investment contract securities to circulate through securities intermediaries, and prioritizing the issuance of such securities in the form of security tokens. Investment contract securities refer to securities in which investors jointly invest in a specific undertaking and receive returns based on profits. Previously, due to their special rights structure, they could only be directly recruited by the issuer and could not enter the public trading market, limiting investment vitality in fields such as art and animal husbandry. The new regulations incorporate investment contract securities into the standardized securities circulation system, requiring them to comply with the same registration, information disclosure, and licensing rules as traditional securities: unlicensed institutions are prohibited from engaging in security token intermediary business, and issuers must fulfill their obligation to fully disclose information to ensure investors' right to know. This adjustment both improves the market accessibility of non-standardized assets and controls risks through the existing regulatory framework, forming a balanced mechanism of "innovation expansion + compliance constraints." (III) Implementation Path of Gradual Advancement and Cross-Institutional Collaboration To avoid market turmoil, the revised bill will take effect one year after its promulgation (expected in January 2027). Before taking effect, the construction of distributed ledger account management infrastructure and the formulation of investor protection details must be completed. The Financial Services Commission (FSC) of Korea will lead the establishment of a joint consultation mechanism, with members including financial regulators, settlement institutions, industry associations, market participants, and academic experts. The first meeting is planned for February 2026, and three working groups will be established for technical infrastructure, issuance regulation, and circulation regulation to refine operational rules. This model of "legislation first + detailed rules improvement + multi-party collaboration" reflects South Korea's prudent balance between financial innovation and risk control. II. Asset Tokenization Legislation and Implementation Practice in Hong Kong, China As an international financial center, Hong Kong, relying on the regulatory logic of "openness + strict access," has constructed an asset tokenization regulatory system centered on sandbox testing and with collaboration between two regulatory agencies. This forms a "pilot-first" model, different from South Korea's "legislative embedding" approach. Its practical results and institutional design provide important references for regional markets. (I) A Tiered Regulatory Framework with Division of Labor and Differentiated Regulation between Two Institutions in Hong Kong Hong Kong has established a comprehensive regulatory framework through a division of labor and collaboration between the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) and the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA): Security-type RWAs are strictly subject to the Securities and Futures Ordinance and must comply with traditional securities regulatory requirements such as licensed operation and information disclosure; non-security-type RWAs are incorporated into the financial innovation framework, with the HKMA leading risk assessment and compliance guidance. This tiered model ensures regulatory consistency for securities assets while reserving flexibility for innovation in non-standardized assets. Meanwhile, Hong Kong has established a Virtual Asset Service Provider (VASP) licensing system, requiring all virtual asset trading platforms targeting retail investors to operate with a license. This system clarifies core requirements such as capital requirements, cold wallet storage ratios, and liability for damages, controlling risk from the entry point. This system echoes South Korea's account management agency system, both strengthening regulatory transparency through intermediaries. (II) Core Practices of Parallel Sandbox Testing and Policy Incentives The regulatory sandbox is the core mechanism for Hong Kong to promote RWA innovation. Through platforms such as the "Ensemble Project," companies can test innovative models such as cross-border RWA issuance and cross-chain transfers in a controlled environment. Ant Financial's RWA (Renewable Energy Asset) project, a collaboration with Langxin Group, was selected as one of the first pilot projects. Employing a "SPV + Hong Kong Licensed Trust" structure, it embeds equipment ownership confirmation and geographical restrictions, validating a compliant path for the tokenization of real economy assets. The "Digital Bond Grant Scheme," launched in November 2024, provides eligible companies with up to HK$2.5 million in grants, effectively reducing innovation costs and stimulating market participation. In terms of practical achievements, Hong Kong has formed a diversified RWA application ecosystem: In August 2025, Guotai Junan International and Ant Financial jointly issued structured product tokens, realizing cross-chain circulation on Ethereum through a "two-chain, one-bridge" architecture, opening up a connection channel between mainland assets and the global market; HashKey and BOC Prudential jointly issued HK$100 million green bond RWA, lowering the investment threshold to HK$10,000, attracting more than 200 retail investors to participate, and increasing on-chain activity by 300% compared to the traditional market. These cases demonstrate Hong Kong's unique value as a "hub for mainland assets to go global" and also provide practical basis for optimizing regulatory rules. III. Comparison of Future Development Trends of Regulations in South Korea and Hong Kong, China Both South Korea and Hong Kong aim to promote the compliant development of asset tokenization. However, due to differences in institutional foundations and market positioning, they have formed two major paths: "legislation-led" and "pilot-driven." Their future development trends will exhibit a coexistence of collaboration and competition, with core differences concentrated in three dimensions. (I) Balancing Institutional Certainty and Innovative Flexibility South Korea has adopted a "core law embedding" approach, comprehensively incorporating security tokens into the existing regulatory system through revisions to core laws. This approach offers significant advantages in legal certainty and judicial predictability, facilitating institutions' deployment of compliant projects in the medium to long term. However, this model also suffers from insufficient flexibility. Subsequent minor adaptations to the characteristics of blockchain technology may require a longer legislative cycle, limiting its responsiveness to rapidly iterating market innovations. In the future, South Korea will likely optimize institutional flexibility through working group guidelines, focusing on resolving issues such as the connection between on-chain data and regulatory reporting, and the judicial determination of smart contract disputes. Hong Kong adopts a gradual approach of "sandbox pilot + rule improvement," accumulating practical experience through pilot projects and then optimizing regulatory rules in reverse. This approach demonstrates high flexibility for innovation and can quickly adapt to new scenarios such as cross-border RWA and cross-chain transactions. However, frequent rule adjustments may lead to market instability, and the boundaries of investor protection need to be continuously clarified. In the future, Hong Kong will deepen its regulatory sandbox cooperation with financial centers such as Singapore and Dubai, promote mutual recognition of regional regulatory standards, accelerate the refinement of rules for non-security RWA, and strengthen coordination and integration with the mainland financial market. (II) Differences in Regulatory Emphasis on Risk Control and Market Incentives South Korea's regulatory approach prioritizes risk control, reflecting a strong regulatory logic from legislative design to implementation: strengthening intermediary responsibilities through account management institutions, strictly adhering to traditional securities information disclosure and licensing requirements, and preventing RWA business from triggering systemic risks. This model aligns with South Korea's administratively driven financial regulatory culture. Future efforts will focus on improving investor protection details, including risk warnings and loss compensation mechanisms, while exploring collaborative application scenarios between local currency stablecoins and RWA to build a closed-loop ecosystem. Hong Kong adheres to the principle of "balancing innovation incentives with risk control," reducing innovation costs through policy subsidies and sandbox exemptions, while controlling risks through strict entry barriers and penetrating supervision. As an international financial center, Hong Kong will focus more on cross-border RWA business in the future, promoting the standardization of cross-chain architectures such as "two chains and one bridge," exploring the application of central bank digital currencies (CBDCs) in RWA settlement, and strengthening its position as a global asset allocation hub. (III) Ecosystem Development Direction of Local Adaptation and International Collaboration South Korea's asset tokenization ecosystem will rely on local conglomerates and fintech companies, focusing on developing scenarios closely integrated with the real economy, such as art and livestock sectors covered by investment contract securities, and the tokenization of trust beneficiary certificates. Simultaneously, South Korea will promote the transformation of traditional financial institutions, forcing securities firms and custodians to upgrade from channel business to compliance consulting and technology integration services, cultivating new intermediary models. Hong Kong, leveraging its advantages in international capital aggregation, focuses on the tokenization of highly liquid assets, such as green bonds and structured products, attracting global institutional participation. In the future, Hong Kong will deepen its cooperation with the mainland, explore pathways for the tokenization of mainland assets in Hong Kong, and simultaneously promote the alignment of RWA tax and accounting rules with international standards to resolve compliance barriers in cross-border transactions, building an ecosystem of "global assets + Asian liquidity". IV. Multidimensional Impact on RWA Business in South Korea and Hong Kong The deepening of South Korea's legislative amendments and Hong Kong's regulatory practices will reshape the RWA market landscape in both regions from the three aspects of issuance, trading, and services, creating new business opportunities and competitive dynamics, while simultaneously driving the upgrading of the industry ecosystem. (I) Activating Asset Issuance Supply and Optimizing Issuance Efficiency In South Korea, the enactment of the bill will significantly stimulate the demand for tokenization of non-standardized assets. The liberalization of investment contract securities will promote asset securitization in alternative investment areas such as art and livestock, while the legal status of distributed ledgers will lower the financing threshold for SMEs—through the issuance of security tokens, companies can fragment and split their assets, attracting more investors, while shortening the issuance cycle and reducing registration and settlement costs. It is expected that after the bill takes effect in 2027, RWA issuance in South Korea will expand from traditional assets such as bonds and stocks to diversification, with trust beneficiary certificates and investment contract securities becoming the core growth points in the initial stage. For Hong Kong, South Korea's legislative amendments will force it to accelerate the standardization of RWA issuance rules, especially in areas such as retail investor access and cross-chain issuance architecture, to create a differentiated advantage. Hong Kong's existing experience in green bond tokenization will be further promoted, and the tokenization issuance of SMEs and new assets will continue to grow, relying on the "Digital Bond Grant Scheme." Competition between the two places will drive innovation in issuance mechanisms, such as automatic information disclosure through smart contract execution and real-time on-chain rights confirmation, improving the efficiency and transparency of the issuance market. (II) Enhancing Trading Liquidity and Reconstructing the Trading Ecosystem In the South Korean market, the compliant circulation of security tokens will break the time and space limitations of traditional securities trading, and the real-time traceability of on-chain transactions will improve market liquidity. However, due to strict licensing management, initial trading will be concentrated in licensed intermediaries, and retail investors will need to access the market through compliant channels. The introduction of account management institutions will build a dual system of "on-chain trading + offline supervision," ensuring trading efficiency while preventing abnormal transactions and money laundering risks. In the long term, on-chain trading may drive innovation in the market maker system, forming a liquidity service system for security tokens. The Hong Kong trading market will continue to focus on cross-border liquidity, attracting global capital to participate in local RWA trading through cross-chain architecture and regulatory mutual recognition. Following South Korea's legislative amendments, some international projects seeking high compliance certainty may relocate to South Korea, forcing Hong Kong to strengthen its differentiated advantages, such as expanding its retail investor market and enriching the supply of cross-border RWA products. Simultaneously, competition between the two trading markets will drive the convergence of technical standards, such as on-chain data formats and cross-platform integration protocols, laying the foundation for interoperability in the regional RWA market. (III) Fostering New Service Formats and Driving Institutional Transformation The South Korean service market will undergo structural changes: the channel value of traditional securities firms and custodian institutions will weaken, requiring them to transform into compliance consulting, structural design, and technology integration service providers, offering issuers integrated services such as blockchain system integration and smart contract development; blockchain infrastructure service providers will become core participants, undertaking functions such as distributed ledger operation and maintenance and on-chain data verification, ushering in development opportunities. In addition, legal, auditing, and other professional service institutions will expand into new business areas around the needs of dispute resolution for security tokens and on-chain data auditing. Hong Kong's services market will leverage its advantages as an international financial center to strengthen its cross-border service capabilities, such as providing global issuers with Hong Kong-licensed trusts and cross-border settlement services. Simultaneously, it will promote the integration of fintech with traditional services, fostering new business models such as cross-chain technology services and RWA valuation and pricing. The collaboration between the two service markets will drive the formation of industry standards, such as audit guidelines for asset tokenization and risk assessment models, thereby enhancing the overall professionalism of the industry. V. Seizing Regional Opportunities Under Compliance and Innovation South Korea's revision of its two laws to allow security tokens is a significant milestone in the development of asset tokenization in East Asia. Its "legislative embedding" approach complements Hong Kong's "pilot-driven" model, jointly promoting the improvement of the regional regulatory system. According to publicly available data, the global RWA market size has surged from $7.87 billion in the third quarter of 2023 to $32.27 billion in the same period of 2025, a 4.1-fold increase in two years, with the tokenization of traditional assets such as bonds and stocks being the core driving force. Under this trend, regulatory competition and cooperation between South Korea and Hong Kong will provide diversified opportunities for market participants. For institutions, the core opportunity in the South Korean market lies in medium- to long-term strategic positioning under the certainty of compliance, especially in alternative asset tokenization and transformation services for traditional financial institutions. The Hong Kong market, on the other hand, focuses on cross-border liquidity and innovative scenarios, suitable for projects seeking flexibility and global reach. For the industry, the practices in these two locations will drive asset tokenization from "technical experimentation" to "compliant scaling," providing a replicable regional development model for the world.

data.title: Trump, the Fed, and Political Escalation: An Analysis of Inflation, Debt, and Precious Metals
data.description:
Introduction

The intersection of US politics and monetary policy has long been controversial, and recent developments under the Trump administration have pushed this contradiction to a new level. As of January 16, 2026, the conflict between President Trump and Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell highlights deep tensions over economic control, interest rates, and fiscal sustainability. Based on the latest data and historical context, this article analyzes the impact of the Trump administration on the Federal Reserve, the ongoing challenges of inflation and national debt, and the strong demand for gold and silver as safe-haven assets in the context of global de-dollarization.

... The current US economy faces a delicate balance: the Consumer Price Index (CPI) is projected to remain at 2.7% year-on-year in December 2025; national debt interest payments are expected to exceed $1 trillion in 2026; and precious metal prices have reached record highs—gold at approximately $4,619 per ounce and silver at approximately $91.73 per ounce. Global central banks continue to purchase large amounts of gold, with net purchases exceeding 634 tons in 2025 (as of the third quarter), and an estimated average of around 70 tons per month in 2026. These trends indicate a structural shift in the global financial landscape, with domestic political pressures clashing with international reshaping. Trump's conflict with the Federal Reserve: A challenge to monetary independence. The dispute between Trump and the Federal Reserve originated during his first term but escalated sharply in 2026. Trump repeatedly criticized Powell for maintaining high interest rates, arguing that it stifled economic growth and increased the burden on the public. In January 2026, the U.S. Department of Justice launched a criminal investigation into Powell, ostensibly focusing on his statements in his June 2025 congressional testimony regarding a $2.5 billion renovation project for the Federal Reserve headquarters.
