The project reportedly ballooned from an initial estimate of $1.9 billion to $2.5 billion due to the discovery of lead paint and asbestos. Powell's testimony emphasized the absence of luxury amenities (such as a VIP restaurant or new marble), stating it was merely maintenance of a historic building. However, internal documents revealed plans for such projects in 2021, leading to allegations of perjury. In a rare video statement on January 11, 2026, Powell denied wrongdoing, calling the investigation a "pretext" with the real purpose of pressuring for interest rate cuts. He stated, "This new threat is not about my testimony last June or the building renovations, but about the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on the public interest, not the president's preferences." This marks the first time a sitting Federal Reserve chairman has faced a criminal investigation, prompting support from central bank governors worldwide, including the European Central Bank and the Bank of Japan, who issued a statement of "full support" on January 13. Furthermore, Trump's attempt to fire Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook in October 2025 (due to a mortgage dispute) has been appealed to the Supreme Court, with a debate scheduled for January 21, 2026. Trump has publicly demanded Powell's resignation, calling him a "stupid" and threatening to replace him within months. This conflict challenges the statutory independence of the Federal Reserve established in 1913, designed to insulate monetary policy from short-term political interference. Historical precedents, such as Nixon's pressure on Arthur Burns leading to inflation in the 1970s, demonstrate that undermining credibility can trigger inflationary expectations and market volatility. In 2026, the Fed's benchmark interest rate will be 4.75%-5%. Further rate cuts could stimulate growth, but the risk of reigniting inflation, especially given Trump's proposed tariffs and fiscal expansion, remains. Economists warn that weakening the Federal Reserve's independence could lead to "runaway inflation expectations, macroeconomic instability, and increased financial volatility." Market reactions were mixed: a week after the survey's release, US stocks fell slightly, gold and silver hit record highs, and the dollar weakened slightly against major currencies, reflecting investors' concerns about hedging against the instability of the US system. If the survey continues, it could delay the Fed's nomination process and complicate policy decisions, exacerbating economic risks. Inflation, Interest Rate Cuts, and the Debt Trap. The core of the Trump-Fed dispute lies in the inflation and debt predicament facing the US economy. December 2025 CPI annual rate is 2.7%, unchanged from November; core CPI (excluding food and energy) annual rate is 2.6%, lower than the expected 2.7%. Monthly CPI rose 0.3%, driven by housing and food costs. Inflation stems from post-pandemic stimulus, supply chain disruptions, and fiscal expansion. The Federal Reserve aggressively raised interest rates starting in 2022 to address a peak inflation of 9.1%, but progress stalled. Powell's cautious stance clashes with Trump's demands for significant interest rate cuts (to stimulate 3-4% GDP growth); rapid easing could exacerbate price pressures, as in the 1970s. The national debt crisis intensifies. As of January 2026, the total U.S. debt was approximately $38.43 trillion, with interest payments in fiscal year 2025 nearing $970 billion, almost three times the $345 billion in 2020. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) projects net interest income to reach $1 trillion in 2026, exceeding Medicare spending and representing 3.2% of GDP, higher than the peak in 1991. By 2035, it could reach $1.8 trillion, representing 4.1% of GDP. This "debt vicious cycle" stems from rising interest rates requiring more borrowing, while foreign demand declines (partly due to the weaponization of the dollar, such as the freezing of Russian assets after 2022). With the proportion of foreign holdings of U.S. debt declining, the CBO warns that without fiscal constraints, the debt-to-GDP ratio could reach 122% by 2035, risking a sovereign debt crisis. Trump's policies—tax cuts, tariffs, and infrastructure spending—may widen the deficit, projected to average $2 trillion annually. Tariffs are intended to protect domestic industries, but could drive up import costs; analysis by the Peterson Institute for International Economics suggests that 10-20% tariffs could increase inflation by 1-2%. In this context, inflation becomes a policy tool rather than a flaw. It erodes debt value, benefiting debtors like the government, but harming savers and those with fixed incomes. History shows that controlling inflation devalued debt, but the risk of it spiraling out of control led to hyperinflation. Why are Gold and Silver Soaring? Amidst uncertainty, gold and silver have become top safe-haven assets. As of January 16, 2026, the spot price of gold was approximately $4,619 per ounce, an annual increase of about 70%; silver was approximately $91.73 per ounce, an annual increase of nearly 200%. This parabolic rise reflects domestic turmoil in the United States and global trends. The main driver is institutional demand, especially from central banks. Data from the World Gold Council shows that net gold purchases in the first three quarters of 2025 totaled 634 tons, lower than the annual average of over 1,000 tons in the previous three years, but far exceeding the average of 400-500 tons before 2022. Poland and Kazakhstan were among the top buyers. J.P. Morgan predicts gold purchases of 755 tons in 2026. Central bank gold purchases highlight gold's role as a hedge against fiat currency volatility and geopolitical risks. The weaponization of the US dollar is accelerating. The freezing of Russian reserves in 2022 prompted countries to seek risk assets without counterparties, leading to an increase in the proportion of gold reserves. China's unreported accumulation may make it the world's largest holder. Physical demand exceeds the paper market, causing premiums to rise. The gold-silver ratio is around 51, indicating that silver is relatively undervalued, with both industrial uses (solar energy, electronics) and monetary attributes. Manipulation allegations persist in futures markets (such as COMEX), but the physical premium suggests decoupling. US political instability amplifies the effect: Fed investigations drive up demand for gold as a hedge. AI growth, energy transition, and fiscal expansion benefit physical assets. BRICS, de-dollarization, and parallel systems. De-dollarization poses a structural challenge to US hegemony, and the BRICS initiative (Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa, etc.) is accelerating. After its expansion in 2024, BRICS will account for 45% of the global population and 35% of global GDP (purchasing power parity). Trends include local currency settlement: 25-30% of China-Brazil trade will be settled in RMB/Reais, Russian energy in Rubles, and Indian oil in Rupees (worth $13-15 billion). The 2025 BRICS summit will express concerns about US tariffs while simultaneously promoting blockchain payment systems. China is promoting gold settlement through the Belt and Road Initiative and the Shanghai Gold Exchange, bypassing SWIFT. J.P. Morgan points out that the share of emerging market central banks' gold reserves has doubled to 9% in ten years. The 2025 Venezuelan crisis (US intervention and capture of the president) is polarized: some see it as a deterrent to BRICS, while others believe it will accelerate de-dollarization. Gaining access to Venezuelan oil enhances US energy security, but alienates the Global South, prompting faster diversification. Risks: A multipolar system could fragment finance and increase volatility. However, the US dollar still accounts for 58% of global reserves (IMF data). Complete de-dollarization is gradual, but the trend benefits alternatives like gold, which may be significantly revalued during a crisis. Preservation vs. Appreciation: Physical Metals and Mining Stocks

In times of uncertainty, wealth protection strategies fall into two categories: physical metals and mining equity. Physical gold and silver serve as insurance against fiat currency devaluation, with no counterparty risk. Price relative—today, gold at $4619 is "expensive," but historical revaluations (such as the US in 1934) show significant upside potential for failed currencies.

Mining stocks offer leverage: for example, Hecla Mining has risen 400% from its 2025 low, outperforming silver by 175%. High-quality producers amplify profits through output, but risks include operational and market manipulation.

Mining stocks offer leverage: for example, Hecla Mining has risen 400% from its 2025 low, exceeding silver by 175%. High-quality producers amplify profits through production, but risks include operational and market manipulation.

Conclusion

Trump-Fed escalation reflects broader economic cracks: political interference threatens institutional stability, and inflation, debt, and de-dollarization are reshaping global finance. The surge in gold and silver reflects hedging risks, with central bank gold purchases and the BRICS initiative accelerating change.

As of January 16, 2026, the outcome remains uncertain—investigation resolution, interest rate path, and geopolitical shifts will determine the trajectory. However, the trend points to higher volatility, favoring physical assets and adaptive strategies. In times of rising uncertainty, positioning resilience is crucial.

data.title: Trump says Greenland agreement framework formed; Galaxy launches crypto hedge fund.
data.description:
Headline

▌Trump: Framework for Agreement on Greenland Reached; Tariffs Won't Take Effect

US President Trump stated on social media that, based on a very productive meeting with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, we have initially established a framework for a future agreement on Greenland and the entire Arctic region. If this agreement is finalized, it will be a major benefit to the United States and all NATO member states. Based on this understanding, I will not implement the tariffs originally scheduled to take effect on February 1st. Further discussions are also underway regarding Greenland and the Golden Dome project. More information will be provided as negotiations progress. Vice President Vance, Secretary of State Rubio, Special Envoy Witkov, and other relevant personnel will be in charge of these negotiations—they will report directly to me.

▌Galaxy Plans to Launch $100 Million Hedge Fund in Q1, Aiming to Profit from Digital Asset Price Volatility

Mike Novogratz's cryptocurrency group Galaxy plans to launch a $100 million hedge fund in the first quarter of this year. The fund has already secured $100 million in investment from family offices, high-net-worth individuals, and large institutions, aiming to profit from the volatility of digital asset prices. The fund will invest up to 30% of its assets in crypto tokens, with the remainder invested in financial services stocks impacted by digital asset technology and law. Galaxy stated it will provide seed funding for the fund. Galaxy currently manages $17 billion in digital assets. Joe Armao, head of the hedge fund, stated that the fund can profit by screening disruptors and companies of varying quality within the financial services sector. Bitcoin is currently priced at approximately $90,000.

... As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $89,789.10, a 24-hour change of +1.7%; ETH price is $3,003.53, a 24-hour change of +2.0%; BNB price is $885.64, a 24-hour change of -0.1%. SOL price is $130.08, up 3.2% in the last 24 hours; DOGE price is $0.1273, up 3.2% in the last 24 hours; XRP price is $1.96, up 3.6% in the last 24 hours; TRX price is $0.2992, up 0.7% in the last 24 hours. WLFI price is $0.1696, a 24-hour change of +5.5%; HYPE price is $21.63, a 24-hour change of +2.4%. **Policy:** Trump: Hopes to sign the cryptocurrency bill as soon as possible. Congress is studying the regulatory structure of the cryptocurrency market. According to Crypto in America, the U.S. Senate Agriculture Committee is expected to release the latest text of its cryptocurrency market structure bill in preparation for its committee review meeting next Tuesday. This release will reveal key points of consensus reached by both parties after two weeks of additional negotiations. Major points of contention in the bill include whether memes should be included in the definition of "digital goods," overall listing standards, the classification of different tokens, regulatory funding from the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, and ethical provisions. A strong bipartisan agreement by the Agriculture Committee could pave the way for similar legislation by the Senate Banking Committee. Meanwhile, the Banking Committee's review, postponed since last week, has yet to set a new date. Pressure is currently focused on Coinbase, whose sudden withdrawal of support for the bill caused the review to pause; it now needs to reach an agreement with the banking industry on stablecoin yield terms to bring all parties back to the negotiating table. The executive director of the White House Crypto Council hinted on social media that delaying legislation could lead to harsher regulation from a less cryptocurrency-friendly government in the future.

▌David Sacks: Banks Will Fully Enter the Cryptocurrency Sector After the Market Structure Act Passes

According to market news: White House AI and crypto director David Sacks stated that after the passage of the Crypto Market Structure Act, banks will fully enter the cryptocurrency sector.

▌Russian Constitutional Court Rules Crypto Assets Protected by Property Rights, USDT Lending Case Becomes Key Setback

The Russian Constitutional Court ruled on a 2023 USDT lending dispute, clarifying that crypto assets should be protected by property rights laws and finding no legal basis to deny the property rights of crypto asset holders. The ruling pointed out that the lower court's refusal to provide judicial relief on the grounds that "stablecoins are not within the scope of current digital financial asset legislation" was improper; failure to declare crypto assets to tax authorities also does not constitute a reason to deny property rights protection, and the relevant declaration obligation only applies to miners.

Legal experts believe this ruling sets an important precedent for civil and criminal cases involving digital assets and urges legislatures to expedite the clarification of the legal attributes of crypto assets.

Blockchain Applications

▌OKX Wallet Launches New Smart Account System, Enhancing On-Chain Trading Experience

According to official news, OKX Wallet smart accounts are now fully open. Based on TEE technology, this smart account builds an isolated and secure environment within the device, while user assets remain in self-custodial mode, improving user experience while ensuring security. In terms of trading functionality, the smart account supports various advanced trading strategies using the OKX DEX aggregator on the EVM and Solana networks, including Copy Trade, Limit Order, and automatic take-profit/stop-loss (TP/SL). When preset conditions are triggered, the system will automatically execute the transaction on-chain after signing by TEE.

In addition, Smart Account integrates capabilities such as Scanner and Tracker to help users discover trading opportunities and track on-chain addresses. Users can experience this by updating the App to version 6.155 or manually enabling Smart Account mode on the wallet management page. Cryptocurrency ▌Trump: Striving to Ensure the U.S. Remains the World's Crypto Capital According to market news, U.S. President Trump stated, "I am working hard to ensure the U.S. continues to be the world's cryptocurrency capital." ▌Ark Invest: Tokenized Assets May Exceed $11 Trillion by 2030 Cathie Wood's Ark Invest predicts that tokenized assets will grow rapidly over the next decade, and the market size is expected to exceed $11 trillion by 2030 as more traditional financial instruments move on-chain. ARK Invest: Bitcoin Market Cap to Reach $16 Trillion by 2030 Cathie Wood's ARK Invest, in its "Big Ideas 2026" report, predicts that the total market capitalization of the crypto market will expand to approximately $28 trillion by 2030, with Bitcoin accounting for approximately $16 trillion, or about 60%–70%. Based on a fixed supply of 21 million Bitcoins, the price of Bitcoin could reach approximately $760,000, an increase of about 7.6 times from the current price. ARK believes that Bitcoin is consolidating its status as a "digital gold" store of value, benefiting from institutional participation, spot ETFs, corporate treasury allocations, and declining volatility; currently, US spot Bitcoin ETFs and listed companies collectively hold approximately 12% of the Bitcoin supply. The report also predicts that smart contract platforms will become a major growth engine besides Bitcoin, with a combined market capitalization potentially reaching approximately $6 trillion by 2030. BlackRock Deposits 635 BTC and 30,827 ETH to Coinbase According to Onchain Lens, BlackRock has deposited 635.16 BTC (worth approximately $56.64 million) and 30,827.68 ETH (worth approximately $91.45 million) into Coinbase, and may continue to deposit more assets. Strive to Issue $150 Million in Preferred Stock to Purchase More Bitcoin Bitcoin treasury company Strive announced it will issue $150 million in preferred stock to purchase more Bitcoin.

▌UK executive search firm XCE increases its holdings by 10 Bitcoins

According to market news: UK executive search firm @XCEofficial (XCE, stock code $XCE) has increased its holdings by 10 Bitcoins through BTC-Bond, bringing its total Bitcoin holdings to 51.36.

▌Solana Mobile's native token SKR is now listed on Jupiter

Jupiter announced on its X platform that Solana Mobile's native token SKR is now listed on Jupiter. It can be traded this week on Jupiter's web, mobile, and desktop platforms.

... **Important Economic Developments** Morgan Stanley states that while the dollar's role in the global system is being gradually and steadily weakened, gold has become the biggest challenger to the dollar in an increasingly multipolar world due to the limited availability of credible alternative currencies. According to Morgan Stanley's research, the dollar's international influence has declined across several indicators, including a decrease in its share of central bank foreign exchange reserves and a reduction in its use in corporate and emerging market sovereign debt issuance. Despite this, the dollar still holds the largest share of global reserves, suggesting that no truly significant challenger has yet emerged. However, the situation changes when gold is taken into account. Gold's share of central bank assets has risen from approximately 14% to 25%-28%, and this upward trend shows "no signs of slowing down." Risk premiums and hedging activities will continue to put pressure on the US dollar, while supporting gold demand. Furthermore, the role of policy factors in promoting "de-dollarization" is currently in a state of "neutral to slightly accelerating," and the short-term evolution of these policies will determine how far the de-dollarization trend will ultimately go. According to foreign media reports, US President Trump spoke about the Federal Reserve in his speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos. Trump clearly stated that his nominee for Federal Reserve Chairman will be a man. This is consistent with the currently reported shortlist: White House economic advisor Kevin Hassett, Federal Reserve Governor Chris Waller, former Federal Reserve official Kevin Warsh, and BlackRock executive Rick Reid. Trump stated, "I will announce a new Federal Reserve Chairman in the near future. He will be a man. I think he will do a very good job and will be a highly respected person." He added, "Every Federal Reserve Chairman candidate has a great interview. But the problem is that once he takes office as Federal Reserve Chairman, he changes his stance, which is somewhat disloyal. The changes a person can make once they hold the position of Federal Reserve Chairman are astonishing. In short, Federal Reserve Chairman Powell has been too slow in his actions on interest rates. Hopefully, the new Federal Reserve Chairman will do a good job."

▌Trump: Only One Fed Chair in Mind May Be Left, Hopes for Someone Like Greenspan

In an interview in Davos, Trump said, "I would say the candidates for Fed Chair have been narrowed down to three, no, two. And I might tell you, there may only be one left in my mind." Trump indicated that he hopes the chosen Fed Chair will be like former Chairman Alan Greenspan. Trump pointed out that Rick Reid and Kevin Warsh are both good candidates. Regarding Kevin Hassett, Trump said, "Actually, I'd prefer him to stay in his current position."

▌95% Probability of Fed Maintaining Rates in January

According to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by 25 basis points in January is 5%, and the probability of maintaining rates unchanged is 95%.

The probability of a cumulative 25 basis point rate cut by March is 17.1%, the probability of maintaining the current rate is 82.2%, and the probability of a cumulative 50 basis point rate cut is 0.7%. The crypto industry is keen to debate the valuation logic of public chains, and there are good reasons behind this. In the $3 trillion total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies, public chain tokens still account for approximately 84%, making their valuation crucial. Although the industry claims to have entered the "mainstream" stage, even the top investors cannot reach a consensus on public chain valuation. One group consists of investors who adhere to fundamental investment principles, emphasizing the importance of tangible cash flow indicators and price-to-earnings ratios (P/E). The other group consists of staunch supporters of Bitcoin and Ethereum, who believe in the "currency premium" theory. Is there a valuation model that satisfies both sides? Can we incorporate rational economic analysis while avoiding vague conceptual debates like "currency premium"? A valuation model proposed by Jon Ma, CEO of Artemis, seems to achieve this goal. This model is essentially a fusion of two classic valuation methods: 1. A hybrid valuation framework used by venture capital firms to evaluate early-stage technology network platforms (such as Uber and Amazon); 2. The quantity theory of money (MV = PQ). Jon Ma's model seems to have found a reasonable balance between the two major groups of investors, although they will likely still find many points of disagreement. If you are a value investor, this model provides the dual assurance of a price-to-earnings ratio framework and balance sheet discipline, giving you more confidence when investing. If you do not accept real economic value indicators, believe in the currency premium theory, or believe that BlackRock's BUIDL tokenized fund will greatly benefit your crypto assets, then this model also fully respects the structural value of tokenization and network effects. If you are a long-term cryptocurrency investor and agree with Haseeb's belief in "power-law growth," this model also leaves ample room for exponential growth. But most importantly, it's a valuation model that Wall Street can understand and accept.

data.title: Who will be the true source of Alpha in 2026?
data.description:
Author: Nikka WolfDAO

A Class Differentiation Is Underway

Are you all in on RWA and other institutional narratives, or continue to gamble on 100x quick money in Meme/Privacy? This choice may determine whether you reap the rewards or suffer losses in 2026.

Wall Street is betting on a "tokenization revolution." Bernstein predicts that 2026 will mark the start of a tokenization supercycle, and Grayscale even proclaims that RWA (Real-World Assets) will achieve a thousandfold growth by 2030. The institutional script is clear: real estate, bonds, and government bonds are put on-chain, connecting with trillions of dollars of traditional capital.

But the story on the other side of the market is completely different. Meme coin rose 23% at the beginning of the year, and QUAI, a privacy coin that was once suppressed by regulators, surged 261%.

Retail investors in Discord groups are still searching for the "next 100x coin," dismissing the institutional narrative of slow, steady returns. This isn't a simple investment disagreement, but a clash of two worlds: suited institutional investors are calculating RWA's risk-reward ratio in meeting rooms, while retail investors are glued to candlestick charts at 3 AM, chasing Meme coin's price surge. A recent Stocktwits poll shows that 64% of investors believe RWA will deliver the strongest returns in 2026, far exceeding Meme coin's 12% and privacy coins' 8%. But behind this number lies a question—when retail investors start clamoring to allocate RWA, is it a genuine upgrade in understanding, or merely a PTSD reaction following the Meme crash in 2025? RWA: A Promised Land for Institutions, or an Overcrowded Narrow Bridge? Let's first examine why Wall Street is so fascinated by RWA. Currently, the total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies is approximately $3.2 trillion, with Bitcoin fluctuating around $95,000, and the market is searching for its next growth engine. RWA's logic seems flawless: tokenizing traditional assets such as real estate, bonds, and government bonds on the blockchain allows it to enjoy the efficiency advantages of blockchain while connecting to the trillion-dollar capital pool of traditional finance. This isn't speculation, but rather "infrastructure construction." In its "2026 Digital Asset Outlook: The Dawn of the Institutional Era" report, Grayscale listed RWA as a core theme, predicting its size will grow from the current $21 billion level to trillions by 2030. Behind this growth curve is the gradually clarifying regulatory environment—the potential passage of the US CLARITY Act and the fact that US banks have already allowed wealth advisors to allocate 1-4% of their clients' assets to crypto assets. The frontrunners have emerged. Ondo Finance, focusing on RWA lending, currently has a market capitalization of approximately $1.5 billion; Chainlink, as an oracle infrastructure supporting the on-chain storage of RWA data, has a market capitalization exceeding $20 billion. Bernstein has explicitly expressed optimism about these projects and considers Coinbase and MicroStrategy as indirect beneficiaries of the tokenization wave. But here's a paradox: When everyone knows RWA is the "right answer," can it still provide excess returns? RWA's current market capitalization is only around $20 billion, with extremely low liquidity. This means that once institutional funds flood in, the price could indeed surge; but the same logic applies to withdrawals—when the first batch of institutions start taking profits, retail investors may find their RWA tokens unsellable. A more realistic risk is regulatory delays: if the CLARITY Act is delayed, or the Fed's interest rate cut cycle is later than expected, the entire RWA narrative may need to wait another year or two amidst volatility. Institutions can withstand this wait because they have a cost advantage in funding and long-term allocation needs. But can retail investors? This is the most easily overlooked dimension in RWA investment—it not only requires you to judge the direction correctly, but also requires you to have enough patience and liquidity to weather the vacuum period before the narrative materializes. Meme: Cultural Renaissance or Last Stand? Compared to RWA's rational narrative, Meme coin is more like a barometer of crypto market sentiment. In early 2026, the total market capitalization of Meme coins rose from $150 billion at the end of 2025 to $185 billion, an increase of 23%. This figure is far less dramatic than the frenzy of 2021 or early 2025, but it's enough to illustrate one fact: **Meme is not dead.** Base Chain founder Jesse Pollak provides an interesting perspective. He repeatedly emphasizes that Meme is not just a speculative tool, but also "the core of on-chain culture." Through content formats such as images, videos, and music, Memes can attract millions of new users to the blockchain, becoming an entry point for the large-scale adoption of Web3. Meme projects on the Base chain, such as TYBASEGOD, TOSHI, and BLOOFOSTERCOIN, are validating this logic—they are not just tokens, but rather carriers of community culture. However, we must admit that this "cultural argument" is more like finding legitimacy for the platform ecosystem than a genuine investment logic. The essence of Meme coins remains a zero-sum game: early participants profit through community consensus and viral spread, while later entrants pay for liquidity. Only 12% of people in a Stocktwits poll are optimistic about Memes, reflecting the collective avoidance by institutions and rational retail investors. More importantly, the Meme market crash in 2025 left deep scars—many retail investors who bought at the peak are deeply trapped and have yet to recover their losses. So what does the 2026 Meme rebound mean? One interpretation is that the low cost and high throughput of Layer 2 tokens like Base lower the barrier to entry for Meme creation and trading, allowing this sector to maintain activity with smaller capital. However, a more pessimistic interpretation is that this is merely a "zero-sum game"—old investors are profiting at each other's expense, while new funds haven't truly entered the market. If 2026 fails to produce a new 100x miracle (like Shiba Inu in 2021), Memes may gradually become a niche game for a small group of players. Even more alarming is the unlocking selling pressure brought by the concentrated TGE (token generation event) period in Q1. When a large number of tokens are unlocked, market liquidity may be absorbed, and Memes, as the most vulnerable asset class, are often the first to be pressured. This structural risk is rarely discussed in the Meme community, yet it may be a major reason for retail investor losses in 2026. Privacy: The Last Bastion of Idealism, or the Next Target of Regulation? Privacy coins are in the most awkward position in this discussion. On the one hand, Quai Network surged 261% at the beginning of the year, from $0.03 to $0.11, with a market capitalization of approximately $86 million; Monero and Zcash also saw rebounds of 10-50%. On the other hand, the entire privacy coin category has a market capitalization of only around $5-10 billion, almost negligible in the total crypto market capitalization of $3.2 trillion. Privacy coin proponents will tell you this is because they represent the original intention of cryptocurrencies—financial freedom and transaction privacy; privacy coins adhere to the principles of decentralization and censorship resistance. Quai's surge was partly driven by the launch of new mining rigs and DEX liquidity support, demonstrating that technological upgrades can still create short-term alpha for privacy coins. However, reality is far harsher than the ideal. The EU's anti-money laundering laws are tightening regulations on privacy coins, and several exchanges have already delisted coins like Monero. A Stocktwits poll showed only 8% of people are optimistic about privacy coins, reflecting not only regulatory concerns but also a lack of sufficient market consensus on privacy as an investment theme. A deeper question is: can privacy and compliance coexist in the current regulatory environment? If not, privacy coins will either be marginalized as tools for geeks and the dark web, or compromise into "pseudo-privacy" projects. This dilemma makes it difficult for privacy coins to become mainstream investment targets. Of course, for true privacy believers, this presents a long-term opportunity for strategic positioning—buying at historical lows after regulatory changes are finalized could yield substantial returns. However, this requires enduring 3-5 years of volatility and uncertainty, which is too long for most investors. The divergence is a foregone conclusion; where is the Alpha? So, RWA, Meme, and Privacy—which will be the true source of Alpha in 2026? The answer might disappoint you: None of the three, at least not in the way you might imagine. The essence of this divergence is a misalignment of time dimensions—RWA needs to be calculated annually, Meme weekly, and Privacy quarterly. Institutions have the patience to lock in RWA, while retail investors, chasing quick profits, are prone to repeated stop-losses during market fluctuations. The biggest risk isn't "choosing the wrong track," but rather "chasing highs and not being able to hold on." The true Alpha may lie in these two places: 1. The Crossroads: Innovation that Breaks Down Time Misalignment The true Alpha is not at the end of a single track, but at the crossroads of narrative fusion: Privacy-Enhanced RWA: Tokenized bonds and real estate are put on-chain, but transaction intentions and holdings are all encrypted and disclosed only during audits. It transforms from "institutional exclusive" to something that can be played by both "institutions and high-net-worth retail investors." Meme IP Tokenization: Once viral Meme IPs are RWA-ized, they generate cash flow from copyrights, merchandise, and community revenue sharing. It transforms from a "casino" into a "cultural fund."

  • Compliance and Privacy Layer: Default privacy + selective disclosure. Private stablecoins and private cross-chain bridges solve the pain points of institutions (data leaks) and retail investors (being caught off guard).

    • The essence of these crossovers: using institutional patience to feed retail investors' FOMO, and using the viral spread of retail investors to activate institutional liquidity. Pure track players are easily washed out by time misalignment; crossover players can find asymmetric opportunities.

    2. Non-participation: The most underestimated advantage of retail investors

    If you don't understand this differentiation, the best strategy may be not to participate. Hold BTC/ETH and wait for the market to give a clearer signal. FOMO will make you lose money in every track, while patient waiting will allow you to heavily invest at the right time. The biggest advantage of retail investors is flexibility.

January 21 news, according to Binance's official Twitter account, Binance is suspected of launching an AI product, Topic Rush, specifically for meme trading services.

          DeFi Hot Topics

          1. Nansen Launches AI Cryptocurrency Trading Tool

          According to Jinse Finance, Nansen has launched its own cryptocurrency trading tool, enabling users to execute trades through an AI agent and natural language prompts. The platform's functionality has expanded from analysis to trade execution. Nansen announced on Wednesday that this new feature allows users to trade without using traditional charts or order books by entering conversational instructions in its mobile application. The product aims to serve retail users seeking a more convenient channel for trading in the cryptocurrency market. Nansen AI can also analyze on-chain signals, providing data-driven insights to guide investor decisions, and then execute trades in what the platform calls "intuitive trading." The company describes this approach as combining analytics with automation while retaining ultimate control in the hands of the user. According to Jinse Finance, Vitalik Buterin stated that he plans to fully return to decentralized social networks in 2026, believing that a better society requires better large-scale communication tools. These tools should help people filter high-quality information and viewpoints, find consensus, and serve the long-term interests of users, rather than maximizing short-term interactions and emotional conflict. Vitalik points out that there is no "one-size-fits-all" solution to these problems, but enhancing competition is an important starting point, and decentralization is the key path to achieving competition: through a shared data layer, anyone can build different clients on top of it. He revealed that since the beginning of this year, he has been using decentralized social tools for reading and posting, with all content synchronized across platforms such as X, Lens, Farcaster, and Bluesky via Firefly. He also criticized some crypto social projects for deviating from their original purpose, excessively viewing "token issuance" as innovation and attempting to create price bubbles around individuals to incentivize creators. However, practice shows that such models often reward existing social capital rather than content quality, and the tokens have short lifespans. Vitalik emphasized that money and social interaction are not inherently conflicting; the key lies in whether it truly serves the content itself, such as a subscription-based support model, rather than a speculative asset design. In his view, decentralized social interaction should be driven by teams that genuinely care about the "essence of social interaction." Vitalik acknowledged the Aave team's long-term maintenance of Lens and expressed anticipation for the direction of the new team, believing they are more focused on solving real social problems. He stated that in the coming year, he will speak out more on decentralized social platforms and encourage more users to participate in ecosystems like Lens and Farcaster, moving beyond the information-confrontational environment of a single platform and exploring new forms of interaction.

          3. The Kingdom of Bhutan plans to deploy and run the Sei Network validator in the first quarter.

          According to Jinse Finance, the Kingdom of Bhutan plans to deploy and run the Sei Network validator in the first quarter. The validator will be launched through a collaboration between the Sei Development Foundation and the technology department of Drug Holding and Investments (DHI)—Bhutan's main sovereign wealth fund and holding company. Phuntsho Namgay, Head of Innovation and Technology at DHI, stated that as part of its digital transformation goals, the wealth fund plans to continue exploring other collaboration opportunities with the Sei Development Foundation.

          ... "This collaboration marks an exciting step forward for Bhutan in strengthening its role in global blockchain innovation, while also opening new avenues for data valuation, scientific advancement, and fintech." 4. Solana Mobile's native token SKR is now listed on Jupiter. According to Jinse Finance, Jupiter announced on its X platform that Solana Mobile's native token SKR is now listed on Jupiter. It can be traded this week on Jupiter's web, mobile, and desktop platforms. 5. Grayscale applies to convert its Near Trust into an ETF. According to Jinse Finance, Grayscale has filed an S-1 document with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, applying to convert the Grayscale Near Trust into a Grayscale Near Trust ETF.

data.title: The inevitable trend of the rise of equity tokens
data.description:
Author: MattyTranslator: Jiahuan, ChainCatcher

In November 2025, more than 5 years after the $UNI airdrop, Uniswap finally activated its fee switch.

This process was delayed for several years and involved repeated governance battles, even witnessing an extremely embarrassing scene in 2024: a 'stakeholder' (widely believed to be an equity investor) blocked a proposal that should have rewarded token holders. Despite this, the UNIFICation proposal ultimately passed with over 62 million votes.

The fact that Uniswap, as the largest DEX in the crypto space, has taken so long to figure out how to reward its token holders speaks volumes about the current state of equity versus token relations. While UNI token holders theoretically "own" the protocol, they can only watch from the sidelines as equity investors reap all the value from front-end fees. While Uniswap is a prime example of this equity versus token divergence, this problem has been festering for years, affecting virtually every protocol that consistently generates revenue. Equity holders and token holders frequently compete for the same value pool while operating under fundamentally different legal, governance, and economic frameworks. The solutions proposed within the industry are varied: from completely eliminating equity and transferring all ownership on-chain to going to the other extreme—completely abandoning tokens. Both approaches have their supporters, but also significant drawbacks. Extreme Path One: Complete De-equityization Completely eliminating equity and transferring all ownership concepts on-chain is undoubtedly a theoretical solution. In this vision, smart contracts replace shareholder agreements, on-chain balances replace equity structure tables, and governance tokens replace board voting. Instant settlement. Transparent ownership. What's not to like? A major problem is: unless a company's assets, operations, and customers are entirely on-chain, off-chain court systems will always be the ultimate arbitrators in resolving disputes. You can try to make all your off-chain contracts and protocols reference on-chain logic, but this still doesn't change the fact that off-chain courts are arbitrators, and not everything is within your control and can be transferred to on-chain. For example, I could have a tokenized real estate NFT issued by a smart contract claiming ownership of the property, but if the off-chain title deed for that land differs, good luck presenting your NFT when the sheriff delivers the eviction notice. (Again, you can take steps to try and ensure that the off-chain title deed matches the on-chain state, but this doesn't negate the fact that off-chain enforcement has priority.) The "no equity, pure token" approach only applies to a small subset of projects: Completely on-chain networks and protocols, such as Bitcoin, some public chains, and fully autonomous DeFi. These projects have no companies, no employees, no servers, and no external dependencies. After all, that's the beauty of Bitcoin in its original form! An uncensorable system and unconfiscable assets. However, this is not feasible for the vast majority of projects (and the vast majority of potential on-chain activity). Web2 and Web2.5 companies own off-chain assets, customers, payments, and operations. Extreme Path Two: Complete Detoxification At the other extreme of the spectrum, some projects (in fact, the vast majority of companies) have decided to completely abandon tokens. They raise equity, build products, and avoid all the headaches that tokens can bring—while sacrificing all the benefits. Benefits: No tokens mean no SEC knocking on your door. No need to worry about whether governance tokens are securities. No need to design token economics, worry about emissions, or explain buyback mechanisms. Costs: Giving up instant settlement, transparent ownership records, cost-efficiency benefits, and the ability to coordinate global community incentives. Traditional equity transfers are expensive, slow to settle, and inaccessible to most potential investors. Gaining equity exposure in private startups remains expensive, inefficient, and opaque. Even in 2026, the processes required to trade public stocks will seem outdated compared to DeFi. Tokens, despite their shortcomings, have the potential to solve these problems. They enable community ownership and user-owned products. To abandon this entirely would be a step backward. To find the optimal balance between these two extremes, we need to understand what equity offers that tokens cannot. What do equity and tokens each offer? 1. Legal Rights and Recourse When you own equity, you have legal standing. You can sue and enforce your rights. If directors breach fiduciary duty or commit fraud, you have an established legal framework to recover your losses. Token holders (with very few exceptions) have virtually no legally recognized rights or protections. They often have to rely on the market to save their investments. While theoretically a company's entire budget can be placed on-chain, allowing founders to delegate every decision to shareholders without legal authorization introduces significant operational inefficiencies and contradicts the initial purpose of investment—trusting the team's vision and capabilities. 2. Formal Governance Control Shareholders elect the board of directors, approve major transactions, and have the right to codify documents. In contrast, governance tokens often create an illusion of control. As Vitalik stated, token governance has serious flaws: low voting rates (<10%), whale manipulation, and a lack of expertise. More often than not, on-chain governance degenerates into a "decentralized theater," where teams can often ignore votes if they don't like the outcome, as execution still requires manual intervention. 3. Legal Clarity of Value Accumulation In mergers and acquisitions, equity holders have a clear legal right to receive profits. As recent cases with Tensor and Axelar have shown, token holders are often completely abandoned, even after the project has been acquired. Due to this strong legal right to profit sharing, stocks are more reliably traded based on multiples of expected future profits. Token valuations, on the other hand, are often purely speculative and lack fundamental support. Even if a project generates revenue, most projects do not reliably route that revenue to token holders due to regulatory risks and fiduciary duty conflicts. While off-chain protocols can be built to simulate this right, this is far less reliable than the legal basis of equity. 4. A Broader and Deeper Investor Pool In short, the equity market has a much larger investor pool and greater total purchasing power than the token market. The value of the US stock market alone is more than 20 times that of the entire crypto industry. The value of the global stock market is more than 46 times that of the crypto industry. Projects that choose tokens over equity are actually only reaching 2%-5% of the potential purchasing power they could otherwise access. 2026: The Year of Equity Tokens One thing is certain: from tokenized equity to new forms of on-chain governance, 2026 will be a year of innovation and experimentation with equity tokens. The DTC pilot project (launching in the second half of 2026) will be the first time in the US that participants are allowed to hold tokenized securities interests on a blockchain. This represents a shift of the backbone of the US capital market infrastructure onto the blockchain: Nasdaq has proposed trading tokenized securities. Securitize offers real publicly traded shares with full on-chain legal ownership. Centrifuge and others are tokenizing equity through SEC-registered agents. The integration of traditional financial infrastructure with the blockchain track is no longer a pipe dream—it's happening. For crypto-native projects, Uniswap's five-year journey toward a fee switch serves as a warning. The equity vs. token split won't resolve itself automatically. It requires deliberate design, clear protocols, and structures to resolve conflicts of interest. Ultimately, this divergence stems from regulatory uncertainty and a lack of a legal framework. Whether through the SEC's "Crypto Projects" or the Clarity Act, the US is expected to achieve long-awaited regulatory certainty as early as January. By the end of this year, we will no longer be discussing equity vs. tokens. We will be discussing ownership—transparent, transferable, legally protected, and natively digital ownership.
